core_takeaway: Agent_1's first-speaker framing made Stone-for-Agent_1 part of the coalition baseline before Agent_5 could press its stronger Stone valuation. Agent_5 ultimately echoed the baseline and received Quill, so the first position beat the last by agenda-setting rather than by being the final accepted proposer.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1 opened by claiming Stone as its top priority and proposing a quick Stone/Jewel coalition. Later speakers repeatedly treated Stone->Agent_1 as the stable baseline, while Agent_5, despite valuing Stone most, conceded it was not practical to secure in round 1. A counterweight is that Agent_2 achieved the highest utility and Agent_5 submitted the accepted proposal, but that proposal simply ratified the Agent_1-favorable baseline.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 framed Stone as its top target and asked for a coalition around Stone and Jewel. Agent_4 then crystallized the exact baseline, and Agents 2-5 repeatedly endorsed it. Agent_5 stated Stone was its top asset but accepted that it could not realistically secure Stone in round 1.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All private-thinking entries converged on the same stable baseline: Jewel with Agent_2, Stone with Agent_1, Pencil with Agent_3, Apple with Agent_4, and Quill with Agent_5. Agent_5's private reasoning also treated Agent_1's Stone hold as unlikely to move.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Four of five formal proposals matched the baseline allocation that gave Agent_1 Stone and Agent_5 Quill. Agent_2's alternative concentrated items on Agents 1 and 2, but it failed because it excluded Agents 3-5.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private votes show every agent accepted the baseline because it protected a salient personal asset. Agent_1 even accepted Agent_2's alternative for a higher payoff, but Agents 3, 4, and 5 rejected it for giving them nothing.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section is present in this transcript.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening turn to name Stone as its top target and define the coalition problem around placing Stone and Jewel. Agent_2 immediately acknowledged that framing, helping make Agent_1's Stone claim the public starting point.

evidence.quote: Priorities: Stone (top), then Jewel, then Quill.

mechanisms.explanation: Once Agent_4 articulated the one-item-per-agent baseline, later discussion treated Stone->Agent_1 as part of the low-friction consensus. Agent_1 could then endorse rather than defend the allocation.

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker conceded a stronger Stone claim
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 valued Stone more than Agent_1, but spoke after the baseline had already formed and described securing Stone as impractical. This left Agent_5 accepting Quill while Agent_1 kept Stone.

evidence.quote: Stone is the single most valuable item for me

mechanisms.theme: Private reasoning internalized the same focal allocation
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private-thinking entries show the agents treating the baseline as the safest unanimous path, including keeping Stone with Agent_1. That made later votes mostly confirmations of an already internalized allocation.

evidence.quote: The broadly accepted plan in Round 1 is a baseline allocation
Stone is the next-highest priority for Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Final proposer did not gain from being last
mechanisms.explanation: Although Proposal #5 by Agent_5 was the accepted proposal, it was substantively the same baseline first proposed by Agent_1. The last speaker's formal role therefore did not translate into a better final allocation for Agent_5.

The outcome was not simply any deal that helped Agent_1. Agent_2's alternative would have raised Agent_1's payoff, but agents excluded from the allocation rejected it, showing that broad acceptance still constrained the first-position benefit.

evidence.quote: Proposal 2 deprives me of all items
Gives me zero utility in Round 1
